focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Sleep never came on the ride in, and by the time the bus dropped him on East Fourth, the sky over Reno had gone the color of rubbed nickel and the wind had started coming off the desert with grit in it. Eli stood on the curb with the green document folder under his arm and his duffel between his shoes, counting how many breaths he could take before the left side of his chest pinched. Four before it pinched. Five if he held his shoulders still and lied to himself.

The casino hiring office sat behind the parking garage instead of inside the building where guests saw chandeliers and mirrored escalators. Out back there were loading docks, dented linen carts, and a row of smokers in maintenance jackets passing a lighter down the line like a relay baton. The laundry intake door opened and shut every few seconds, and each time it opened he got a burst of wet heat and detergent so strong it tasted chalky.

A woman in a burgundy security polo took his ID, glanced at the Pennsylvania address, and clipped a paper badge to his shirt that said VISITOR in red block letters. She had a tiny silver hoop through the right side of her nose and chewed cinnamon gum with the tired determination of somebody staying awake on purpose.

"You're for laundry?" she asked.

"Whatever starts fastest."

She snorted once, not meanly. "That answer always means laundry."

She pointed him toward a trailer office with fake wood paneling and a hand-lettered sign that said EMPLOYMENT. The trailer shook each time a truck hit the dock bumpers outside, and each shudder lit up the old crack under his left ribs. Inside, the air conditioner rattled loud enough to compete with the slot-machine spill leaking through the wall.

The woman behind the desk had already split his forms into two piles before...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...new number before midnight.

He stared at the line until the ink feathered into the paper fibers. Promises written to himself had not counted before. But the sentence sat there with the others, equal weight, no flourish.

Outside, a truck downshifted on the highway and the sound rolled across the lot like cloth tearing. In room fourteen someone laughed too loud at a TV host. Pipes clicked in the wall as the springs ran hot under concrete. Eli placed the clean copy of the repayment sequence in the green folder, then slid the folder under the atlas so he would touch both before every shift.

Sleep came in scraps. He lay on top of the blanket with one arm across his sore rib and watched the parking-lot light cut a bright rectangle on the ceiling. Twice he sat up because his body swore there was still a bottle somewhere in the room, close enough to grab. There was not. Whenever his mind started to build speeches for Nora, for June, for the version of himself he kept trying to salvage, he forced it back down to the list: October 7, October 9, October 12, October 15. Dates were narrow. Dates did not let him wander.

At five thirty, before dawn had fully lifted, he got up, showered in water that could not decide between cold and scalding, and dressed for the linen run. After it, he had a six-hour shuttle to the Utah salvage yard and a stack of insurance forms that might turn the totaled car into usable cash. He tucked the folded repayment sequence into his back pocket because forgetting had been his favorite method of escape, and he was done pretending that forgetting was accidental.

In the laundry room, the first machine thudded awake. Steam rose. The sulfur smell pushed through bleach again. He set his palms on the warm metal, listened to the drum pick up speed, and began.
